clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents with a 10-week history of symmetrical swelling and stiffness of both wrists and the small joints of her hands. Morning stiffness lasts approximately 90 minutes. RF is positive at high titre, anti-CCP antibody is positive, ESR is 64 mm/hr, and CRP is 38 mg/L. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia but no erosions. She is newly diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis. Which of the following best describes the most appropriate initial pharmacological approach?
options: Start methotrexate 15 mg weekly plus folic acid 5 mg weekly, combined with prednisolone 30 mg daily tapering over 12 weeks
Start hydroxychloroquine alone and review in 6 months
Start a biologic agent such as adalimumab immediately as first-line therapy
Prescribe NSAIDs alone for symptom control and defer DMARDs until erosions appear on X-ray
Start prednisolone 30 mg daily alone and taper without adding a DMARD
explanation: Current guidelines recommend starting methotrexate (initial dose 15 mg weekly, escalated to 25 mg weekly) plus folic acid concurrently with a bridging course of prednisolone (30 mg daily tapered over 12 weeks) at the time of diagnosis. This combination suppresses inflammation rapidly while the DMARD reaches therapeutic effect. Biologics are reserved for patients who fail conventional DMARD therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. MTX with folic acid and short-course prednisolone is the recommended first-line approach per the management algorithm for new RA.
Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy is insufficient for seropositive erosive RA with high disease activity; combination DMARDs or MTX are required.
Biologic agents are second-line, reserved for patients who fail at least one conventional DMARD such as methotrexate.
NSAIDs address pain but do not prevent joint damage; DMARDs should be started promptly at diagnosis regardless of erosion status.
Prednisolone alone does not prevent joint damage; a DMARD must be added simultaneously as the cornerstone of RA treatment.
clinicalPearl: In new RA, start MTX plus folic acid simultaneously with a short bridging course of prednisolone; never leave the patient on steroids alone.
keyClue: Newly diagnosed seropositive RA with high inflammatory markers — DMARD initiation is mandatory at first diagnosis.
commonExamTrap: Choosing biologic therapy as first-line; biologics are second-line after conventional DMARD failure.
missedConcept: The concurrent bridging steroid regimen alongside DMARD initiation and the mandatory use of folic acid with methotrexate.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis (RF and anti-CCP positive) presents with recurrent bacterial infections and fatigue. He takes methotrexate 20 mg weekly. On examination, there is splenomegaly, joint deformities including ulnar deviation of the fingers, and rheumatoid nodules over the elbows. Blood tests show: Hb 98 g/L, WBC 2.1 x10^9/L (neutrophils 0.9 x10^9/L), platelets 98 x10^9/L, ESR 75 mm/hr. Which complication of RA does this presentation most likely represent?
options: Felty's syndrome
Methotrexate-induced bone marrow suppression
Large granular lymphocyte syndrome unrelated to RA
Amyloidosis presenting as haematological abnormalities
Reactive haematological changes from chronic inflammation alone
explanation: Felty's syndrome is a rare complication of long-standing seropositive RA characterised by splenomegaly, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, and anaemia (SANTA mnemonic). It occurs in patients with high-titre RF and ACPA, is associated with recurrent infections due to neutropenia, and typically appears after many years of poorly controlled disease. The clinical triad of RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia is diagnostic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Felty's syndrome — RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia + thrombocytopenia + anaemia — fits this presentation perfectly.
MTX can cause bone marrow suppression but would not cause splenomegaly and is a diagnosis of exclusion; folic acid co-prescription reduces this risk.
Large granular lymphocyte syndrome can mimic Felty's but the clinical context of 15-year seropositive RA with splenomegaly strongly favours Felty's.
Amyloidosis complicates long-standing RA but presents with nephrotic syndrome or organ dysfunction, not splenomegaly with cytopenias.
Chronic inflammation causes normocytic anaemia but not neutropenia or splenomegaly; a separate process must be considered.
clinicalPearl: Felty's syndrome = RA + Splenomegaly + Anaemia + Neutropenia + Thrombocytopenia (SANTA); always occurs in seropositive, long-standing erosive RA.
keyClue: Long-standing seropositive RA, splenomegaly, and neutropenia with recurrent infections.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the pancytopenia solely to methotrexate toxicity without recognising splenomegaly as the key distinguishing feature.
missedConcept: Felty's syndrome is a distinct extra-articular manifestation of RA, not a drug side effect, and splenomegaly is the pivotal clinical clue.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman with a 7-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis presents to the emergency department with a painful red right eye and photophobia. She reports that the pain is deep and boring in quality, worse at night, and has been present for 4 days. Visual acuity is reduced in the right eye. Slit-lamp examination reveals inflammation of the deep scleral layers. She is currently on methotrexate and hydroxychloroquine. Which ocular complication of RA does this presentation most represent, and what is the clinical priority?
options: Scleritis — a sight-threatening complication requiring urgent ophthalmological review and escalation of systemic immunosuppression
Keratoconjunctivitis sicca — manage with artificial tear drops
Conjunctivitis due to methotrexate toxicity — withhold methotrexate
Episcleritis — self-limiting, reassure and use topical NSAIDs
Anterior uveitis unrelated to RA — refer routinely to ophthalmology
explanation: Scleritis in RA is an uncommon but serious and potentially sight-threatening complication due to granulomatous inflammation of the deep scleral layers. It presents with deep, boring eye pain (worse at night), photophobia, and reduced visual acuity. It requires urgent ophthalmological review and escalation of systemic immunosuppression. In contrast, the most common ocular feature is keratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eyes), which is benign.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Deep boring pain with reduced vision and slit-lamp evidence of deep scleral inflammation is scleritis — a sight-threatening emergency in RA.
Keratoconjunctivitis sicca presents with dry, gritty eyes, not deep boring pain and visual loss; it is managed with lubricant drops.
Methotrexate does not cause acute painful scleritis; this presentation requires systemic assessment and urgent ophthalmology.
Episcleritis is superficial, usually not painful, does not reduce visual acuity, and is self-limiting; scleritis is deeper and more serious.
Uveitis is associated with seronegative spondyloarthropathies rather than RA; the slit-lamp finding of deep scleral inflammation points to scleritis.
clinicalPearl: In RA, dry eyes (KCS) are common and benign; deep boring eye pain with visual loss is scleritis — a sight-threatening emergency requiring urgent ophthalmology.
keyClue: Deep boring pain, photophobia, reduced visual acuity, and deep scleral inflammation on slit-lamp in a patient with RA.
commonExamTrap: Dismissing painful red eye in RA as simple episcleritis or conjunctivitis; visual loss distinguishes the serious from the benign.
missedConcept: Scleritis (not keratoconjunctivitis sicca) is the serious ocular complication of RA and requires urgent systemic treatment escalation.
redFlagReason: Scleritis is a sight-threatening complication of RA; failure to recognise and treat urgently can result in permanent visual loss.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man with established rheumatoid arthritis is reviewed in clinic after 3 months on methotrexate 20 mg weekly. He reports ongoing joint pain in both wrists and multiple finger joints. On examination, 8 joints are tender and 6 are swollen. ESR is 55 mm/hr. His visual analogue scale for global health is 70/100. His DAS28 score is calculated at 5.8. How should this result be interpreted, and what management change does it indicate?
options: High disease activity (DAS28 >5.1) — consider adding a second DMARD such as sulfasalazine and hydroxychloroquine to form triple therapy
Remission (DAS28 <2.6) — continue current therapy unchanged
Moderate disease activity (DAS28 2.6–5.1) — continue current therapy with review in 3 months
The DAS28 score is not valid until the patient has been on therapy for 6 months
High disease activity — immediately start a biologic agent without further DMARD trials
explanation: A DAS28 score greater than 5.1 indicates high disease activity. The management algorithm for RA directs that when DAS28 remains above 2.6 after methotrexate alone, the next step is to add sulfasalazine and hydroxychloroquine (triple therapy). Biologics are considered when DAS28 remains above 5.1 despite adequate triple therapy. DAS28 should be assessed every 3 months to guide treatment decisions.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. DAS28 >5.1 = high disease activity; the next step per the algorithm is to add SSZ + HCQ to form triple therapy before considering biologics.
Remission is defined as DAS28 <2.6; a score of 5.8 is high disease activity and requires treatment escalation.
Moderate activity is DAS28 2.6–5.1; this patient's score of 5.8 exceeds this threshold and represents high activity requiring action.
DAS28 is a valid real-time tool for monitoring disease activity and guiding treatment decisions at every clinic visit.
Biologics are reserved for patients with DAS28 >5.1 who have already failed triple DMARD therapy, not as a second-line step.
clinicalPearl: DAS28 >5.1 = high activity (add DMARD or biologic); 2.6–5.1 = moderate; <2.6 = remission. Assess every 3 months.
keyClue: DAS28 score of 5.8 in a patient on methotrexate monotherapy.
commonExamTrap: Jumping straight to biologic therapy; per the algorithm, triple DMARD therapy (MTX + SSZ + HCQ) should be tried before biologics.
missedConcept: The DAS28 score thresholds and the sequential treatment escalation pathway in RA management.

clinicalStem: A 64-year-old man with a 20-year history of seropositive erosive rheumatoid arthritis presents with progressive neck pain radiating to the occiput and new onset of bilateral hand weakness and difficulty walking over 3 weeks. He denies trauma. On examination, he has hyperreflexia in both lower limbs and a positive Babinski response bilaterally. Hand grip strength is reduced bilaterally. Plain X-rays of the hands show advanced erosive changes. What is the most important next investigation and clinical priority?
options: Urgent lateral flexion and extension cervical X-rays followed by MRI cervical spine to assess for atlantoaxial subluxation with cord compression
Arrange routine outpatient nerve conduction studies for suspected carpal tunnel syndrome
Increase methotrexate dose and review in 4 weeks
Order EMG to investigate peripheral neuropathy as a complication of RA
Arrange urgent CT brain to exclude cerebrovascular disease
explanation: Atlantoaxial subluxation is a rare but life-threatening complication of long-standing RA caused by destruction of the transverse ligament of the atlas and erosion of the odontoid peg, leading to cervical cord compression. Upper motor neurone signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski) in a patient with severe RA should trigger urgent lateral flexion/extension cervical spine X-rays and MRI. Cord compression is a neurosurgical emergency.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. UMN signs in long-standing RA mandate urgent cervical spine imaging to exclude atlantoaxial subluxation with cord compression.
Carpal tunnel syndrome is a common RA complication but causes lower motor neurone signs in the hand; bilateral UMN signs indicate central cord pathology.
Escalating immunosuppression does not address mechanical cord compression; surgical stabilisation may be required urgently.
Mononeuritis multiplex from rheumatoid vasculitis causes lower motor neurone peripheral deficits; bilateral UMN signs localise the lesion to the cervical cord.
Cerebrovascular disease could cause UMN signs but the combination of neck pain and RA with erosive disease makes atlantoaxial subluxation the priority diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: Long-standing RA + neck pain + upper motor neurone signs = atlantoaxial subluxation until proven otherwise; urgent cervical MRI is mandatory.
keyClue: Bilateral upper motor neurone signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski) and hand weakness in a patient with 20-year erosive RA.
commonExamTrap: Attributing hand weakness to carpal tunnel syndrome and missing the bilateral UMN signs that indicate central cord compromise.
missedConcept: Atlantoaxial subluxation is a neurosurgical emergency in RA; UMN signs in any RA patient must prompt urgent cervical spine imaging.
redFlagReason: Atlantoaxial subluxation with spinal cord compression is life-threatening; delayed diagnosis can result in tetraplegia or death.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old woman with a BMI of 34 presents with a 3-year history of bilateral knee pain, worse on walking and stair climbing, relieved by rest. Morning stiffness lasts less than 15 minutes. Examination reveals varus deformity, bony enlargement at the joint line, and coarse crepitus on flexion. X-rays confirm bilateral medial tibio-femoral osteoarthritis. She has no significant cardiovascular or renal disease. Her pain is currently inadequately controlled with paracetamol 1g QID. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Add a topical NSAID gel to the knees, advise structured quadriceps strengthening exercises, and counsel on weight loss
Refer immediately for total knee replacement surgery
Start oral prednisolone 20 mg daily
Prescribe methotrexate as a DMARD for osteoarthritis
Arrange urgent MRI of both knees
explanation: In knee OA, non-pharmacological measures including weight loss (the most effective intervention in obese patients) and quadriceps strengthening exercises are first-line. Topical NSAIDs are more effective than paracetamol for knee OA with fewer systemic side effects. Oral NSAIDs are considered when topical agents are insufficient. Surgery is reserved for patients who fail optimal medical therapy with significant structural damage.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Topical NSAIDs plus quadriceps exercises plus weight loss is the correct stepwise approach for inadequately controlled knee OA on paracetamol.
Surgery is reserved for patients who have failed all conservative and medical measures, cannot walk a block, or cannot climb stairs; this patient has not yet tried NSAIDs.
Oral corticosteroids have no role in chronic OA management and would cause significant harm (osteoporosis, glucose dysregulation) in this age group.
DMARDs such as methotrexate are used in inflammatory arthritis (RA), not in degenerative OA.
MRI is not indicated for straightforward OA; plain X-ray is the primary imaging tool and has already confirmed the diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: Weight loss is the single most effective intervention for knee OA in obese patients; combine with quadriceps strengthening and topical NSAIDs before oral NSAIDs.
keyClue: Obese patient, bilateral knee OA inadequately controlled on paracetamol, no contraindications to NSAIDs.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to joint replacement without escalating medical and lifestyle therapy first.
missedConcept: The stepwise management of OA: lifestyle (weight loss) + exercise + topical NSAID before oral NSAID before surgery.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old retired farmer presents with a 2-year history of progressive left groin pain that radiates to the anterior thigh and occasionally to the knee. The pain is worse with prolonged walking and relieved by rest. He has no morning stiffness. On examination, an antalgic gait is noted. Range of movement testing of the left hip reveals pain and markedly restricted internal rotation with the hip at 90 degrees of flexion; other movements are also reduced but less severely. External rotation deformity is observed. Which of the following best explains these findings and suggests the most likely diagnosis?
options: Hip osteoarthritis — restricted internal rotation with the hip flexed is the earliest and most sensitive sign of hip OA
Trochanteric bursitis — tenderness over the greater trochanter is the hallmark
Lumbar radiculopathy at L3/L4 — nerve root compression causing anterior thigh pain
Rheumatoid arthritis of the hip — symmetrical joint involvement is expected
Avascular necrosis of the femoral head — acute onset and history of steroid use are typical
explanation: In hip OA, pain and restricted internal rotation with the hip flexed at 90 degrees is the earliest and most sensitive clinical sign. Pain is characteristically felt in the anterior groin with radiation to the anterolateral thigh and knee. An antalgic gait and fixed external rotation deformity develop with progressive disease. Occupational history of farming (repetitive loading) is a recognised risk factor for hip OA.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Restricted internal rotation with hip flexed is the hallmark early sign of hip OA; anterior groin pain radiating to the knee is characteristic.
Trochanteric bursitis causes lateral hip pain localised to the greater trochanter, not anterior groin pain with restricted internal rotation.
L3/L4 radiculopathy causes anterior thigh pain but would show neurological deficits and a positive femoral stretch test without hip joint restriction.
RA of the hip would have associated inflammatory features, bilateral involvement, and positive serology; OA is more consistent here.
Avascular necrosis typically presents acutely, often with corticosteroid use, and the history of gradual onset over 2 years in a farmer points to primary OA.
clinicalPearl: In hip OA, painful restriction of internal rotation with the hip flexed is the earliest and most sensitive clinical sign.
keyClue: Anterior groin pain radiating to the knee, antalgic gait, and restricted internal rotation with hip flexed at 90 degrees.
commonExamTrap: Attributing anterior thigh and knee pain to a lumbar problem rather than recognising the hip as the source when internal rotation is restricted.
missedConcept: Hip pain can present as knee pain; always examine the hip when a patient complains of knee pain without clear local findings.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old woman with confirmed right knee osteoarthritis has been prescribed paracetamol, topical diclofenac, and a course of supervised physiotherapy for 3 months. She continues to have moderate-to-severe pain limiting her daily activities. She has a history of peptic ulcer disease and stage 3 chronic kidney disease, making oral NSAIDs high-risk. She is not yet a candidate for total knee replacement as her structural damage is mild on X-ray. What is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Intra-articular glucocorticoid injection into the right knee
Commence oral prednisolone 15 mg daily
Start methotrexate 15 mg weekly
Prescribe codeine phosphate as long-term analgesia
Refer immediately for total knee replacement
explanation: Intra-articular glucocorticoid injections are effective in knee OA and provide short-term pain relief. They are particularly useful in patients in whom oral NSAIDs are contraindicated (CKD, peptic ulcer history). Serial injections every 3 months have shown efficacy for up to 1 year. Total knee replacement requires significant structural damage and failure of all conservative measures.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Intra-articular glucocorticoid is the appropriate next step when oral NSAIDs are contraindicated and conservative measures have failed.
Oral prednisolone has no role in chronic OA and carries significant systemic risks including gastrointestinal bleeding, especially given her peptic ulcer history.
Methotrexate is a DMARD used in inflammatory arthritis; it has no established role in degenerative OA.
Long-term opiates carry significant risks (constipation, falls, dependence) in the elderly; intra-articular injection is a safer and more targeted approach.
Surgery requires significant structural damage on X-ray and failure of all medical therapies; this patient has mild structural changes and has not tried intra-articular injection.
clinicalPearl: Intra-articular glucocorticoids are effective for knee OA flares or when systemic analgesics are contraindicated; can be repeated every 3 months.
keyClue: Oral NSAIDs contraindicated due to peptic ulcer disease and CKD; conservative measures have failed with mild structural damage.
commonExamTrap: Recommending surgery before exhausting intra-articular injection, or prescribing oral NSAIDs despite clear contraindications.
missedConcept: Intra-articular glucocorticoid injections fill the treatment gap between failed oral analgesia and surgical intervention in knee OA.

subtopic: Risk of visual loss in GCA
clinicalStem: A 74-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of severe right-sided temporal headache, jaw pain on chewing, and scalp tenderness. This morning she experienced a brief episode of loss of vision in her right eye lasting 10 minutes, which then resolved. ESR is 98 mm/hr and CRP is 72 mg/L. She has bilateral shoulder girdle stiffness on waking. Fundoscopy is currently normal. What is the single most important immediate management step?
options: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone (40–60 mg daily) immediately, before any further investigations are awaited
Arrange urgent temporal artery biopsy and wait for the result before starting steroids
Refer to ophthalmology for slit-lamp examination as the first priority before any treatment
Start aspirin 300 mg alone pending rheumatology review
Arrange PET scan to confirm GCA before committing to steroid therapy
explanation: Giant cell arteritis presenting with amaurosis fugax (transient visual loss) is an ophthalmological emergency. High-dose prednisolone must be commenced immediately to prevent permanent blindness due to occlusion of the posterior ciliary artery. Treatment should not be delayed to await biopsy results; temporal artery biopsy can be performed up to 2 weeks after starting steroids without significantly affecting diagnostic yield. Response is dramatic within 48–72 hours.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. In GCA with visual symptoms, prednisolone must be started immediately — before biopsy — to prevent irreversible visual loss.
Biopsy is important for diagnosis but must never delay steroid treatment in a patient with visual symptoms; treatment before biopsy is the standard of care.
Ophthalmology review is important but should not precede or delay immediate steroid treatment when transient visual loss has already occurred.
Aspirin alone is insufficient to prevent the ischaemic cascade leading to irreversible blindness in GCA; high-dose prednisolone is the mainstay.
PET scan is a useful investigation in GCA but has no role in acute visual emergency management; steroids must be started immediately.
clinicalPearl: In GCA with any visual symptoms, start high-dose prednisolone immediately — even before biopsy — as waiting risks permanent blindness.
keyClue: Temporal headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, raised ESR/CRP, and transient monocular visual loss in a 74-year-old woman.
commonExamTrap: Waiting for biopsy results before starting prednisolone — this delay can result in permanent, irreversible blindness.
missedConcept: GCA with visual symptoms is a true ophthalmological emergency; steroid treatment must precede all investigations.
redFlagReason: Transient visual loss in GCA indicates imminent occlusion of the posterior ciliary artery; delayed treatment causes permanent blindness.

clinicalStem: A 42-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of recurrent nosebleeds, persistent nasal crusting, purulent nasal discharge, and left-sided deafness. Over the past 6 weeks he has developed haemoptysis, significant weight loss, and night sweats. Urinalysis shows 3+ haematuria and 2+ proteinuria. Chest X-ray reveals bilateral pulmonary nodules. Blood tests show: CRP 88 mg/L, ESR 85 mm/hr, creatinine 198 umol/L. ANCA testing is positive. Which ANCA subtype is most likely to be positive, and what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA) positive — Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener's granulomatosis)
MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA) positive — Microscopic polyangiitis
ANA positive — Systemic lupus erythematosus with renal and pulmonary involvement
RF and anti-CCP positive — Rheumatoid vasculitis
Anti-GBM antibody positive — Goodpasture's syndrome
explanation: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) classically presents with upper respiratory tract involvement (epistaxis, nasal crusting, sinusitis, deafness), lower respiratory tract involvement (haemoptysis, pulmonary nodules), and renal involvement (haematuria, proteinuria, rising creatinine). It is associated with PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA). MPO-ANCA is associated with microscopic polyangiitis, which predominantly affects the kidney and lungs but lacks prominent upper airway features.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA) in a patient with upper airway, pulmonary, and renal involvement is diagnostic of GPA.
MPO-ANCA is associated with microscopic polyangiitis, which has prominent renal and alveolar haemorrhage but lacks the upper airway granulomatous features.
SLE can cause pulmonary-renal syndrome but presents with ANA/dsDNA antibodies, not ANCA; skin and joint features would be expected.
Rheumatoid vasculitis occurs in seropositive RA patients and does not cause upper airway granulomatous disease or pulmonary nodules in this pattern.
Goodpasture's syndrome is anti-GBM antibody-mediated, presents with haemoptysis and nephritis but lacks upper airway involvement and is not ANCA-associated.
clinicalPearl: GPA = upper airway granulomas (epistaxis, sinusitis, deafness) + pulmonary nodules + rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis + PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA).
keyClue: Nasal crusting, epistaxis, deafness, haemoptysis, pulmonary nodules, renal impairment, and positive ANCA.
commonExamTrap: Confusing PR3-ANCA (GPA) with MPO-ANCA (microscopic polyangiitis); remember that upper airway granulomatous disease points to GPA.
missedConcept: The PR3 versus MPO ANCA distinction and the clinical triad of upper airway, lung, and kidney involvement that characterises GPA.

topic: Polymyalgia rheumatica versus giant cell arteritis
clinicalStem: A 69-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of severe bilateral shoulder and hip girdle stiffness, worst on waking, making it impossible to lift her arms above her head or rise from a chair without help. She has no headache, no jaw pain, and no visual symptoms. ESR is 82 mm/hr, CRP is 56 mg/L, and Hb is 104 g/L (normocytic). Muscle enzymes are normal. She has no weakness or muscle tenderness. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Start prednisolone 15–20 mg daily for polymyalgia rheumatica and arrange urgent follow-up to monitor for development of GCA symptoms
Start prednisolone 60–80 mg daily as for giant cell arteritis
Arrange temporal artery biopsy before starting any treatment
Start methotrexate as first-line treatment for PMR
Prescribe NSAIDs alone as PMR symptoms are musculoskeletal in nature
explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) without features of GCA is treated with prednisolone 15–20 mg daily (not the higher dose of 60–80 mg used in GCA). Response is dramatic within 48–72 hours. Patients must be counselled about the risk of developing GCA features and instructed to return immediately if headache, jaw claudication, or visual symptoms develop. Temporal artery biopsy is not required if GCA features are absent.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. PMR without GCA features is treated with prednisolone 15–20 mg daily; higher doses are used when GCA is suspected.
60–80 mg prednisolone is the dose for GCA; using this dose for PMR alone is unnecessary and exposes the patient to excess steroid side effects.
Temporal artery biopsy is indicated when GCA features are present; PMR without headache or visual symptoms does not require biopsy.
Methotrexate is a steroid-sparing agent added when prednisolone cannot be tapered, not as first-line therapy in PMR.
NSAIDs are inadequate for PMR; the dramatic response to prednisolone is in fact a diagnostic hallmark of PMR.
clinicalPearl: PMR responds dramatically to prednisolone 15–20 mg/day; use 60–80 mg/day only when GCA is suspected. Always monitor for GCA development.
keyClue: Bilateral shoulder and hip girdle stiffness, elevated ESR/CRP, normal muscle enzymes, and no headache or visual symptoms — classic PMR.
commonExamTrap: Using the GCA steroid dose (60–80 mg) for isolated PMR, or failing to counsel the patient about GCA warning symptoms.
missedConcept: The steroid dose distinction between PMR (15–20 mg) and GCA (60–80 mg) is a high-yield exam concept.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the GP with a 5-day history of a non-blanching purpuric rash over his buttocks and lower legs, colicky abdominal pain, and swollen ankles. He had a sore throat 2 weeks ago. On examination, the rash is palpable and distributed over the extensor surfaces and buttocks. He is afebrile. Urinalysis shows 2+ blood and 1+ protein. Renal function tests are within normal limits. Which statement best describes the next management step?
options: Monitor closely with serial urinalysis and blood pressure measurements; prescribe analgesics for pain; add glucocorticoids given the presence of nephritis
Commence urgent IV cyclophosphamide as this is ANCA-associated vasculitis
Start oral antibiotics empirically as the rash is infectious purpura
Perform an immediate renal biopsy to guide treatment
Reassure that this is a benign self-limiting rash and no follow-up is required
explanation: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (HSP) is an IgA-mediated small vessel vasculitis triggered by infection (typically streptococcal). It presents with palpable purpura over the buttocks/lower legs, abdominal pain, arthralgia, and nephritis. HSP is usually self-limiting, but nephritis requires close monitoring and glucocorticoids in severe or persistent cases. Nephritis can develop up to 4 weeks after onset and requires serial urinalysis and blood pressure monitoring even after the rash clears.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. HSP with nephritis (haematuria/proteinuria) requires close monitoring and glucocorticoids; most cases are self-limiting but nephritis indicates more severe disease.
Cyclophosphamide is used for organ-threatening ANCA-associated vasculitis (GPA, MPA); HSP is IgA-mediated and not ANCA-associated.
HSP is an immune-mediated vasculitis, not an infectious rash; antibiotics treat the triggering infection if still active but do not treat HSP itself.
Renal biopsy is not required in straightforward HSP; it is reserved for atypical presentations or if renal function deteriorates significantly.
HSP with proteinuria and haematuria indicates nephritis; follow-up is mandatory as nephritis can progress and may require intervention.
clinicalPearl: HSP nephritis can appear up to 4 weeks after the rash; all HSP patients need serial urinalysis for at least 4–6 weeks after presentation.
keyClue: Post-infectious purpura over buttocks and lower legs with abdominal pain, arthralgia, and haematuria/proteinuria in a child.
commonExamTrap: Dismissing HSP as benign because the rash appears to be improving; nephritis can develop late and requires ongoing monitoring.
missedConcept: HSP nephritis can present up to 4 weeks after the skin rash and may progress to renal failure if not monitored and treated.
